title: The Elephant.
content:
This is the El-e-phant, who lives
    With but one aim - to please.
    His i-vo-ry tusk he free-ly gives
    To make pi-a-no keys.
    One grief he has - how-e'er he tries,
    He nev-er can for-get
    That one of his e-nor-mous size
    Can't be a house-hold pet.
    Then does he to his grief give way,
    Or sink 'neath sor-row's ban?
    Oh, no; in-stead he spends each day
    Con-tri-ving some un-sel-fish way
    To be of use to Man.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Oliver Herford represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Oliver Herford's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
